title: The Signature and Natural Slope of Hyperbolic Knots
speaker: Marc Lackenby
abstract: Andras Juhasz has explained in his talk how machine learning was used to discover a previously unknown relationship between invariants in knot theory. This relationship is a connection between a classical knot invariant (the signature) and a new knot invariant (the natural slope) derived from hyperbolic geometry. In my talk, I will explain how this conjecture was formulated, verified in millions of cases, and then eventually disproved, and how two more refined conjectures were proved.

proof_stages.label: Knot invariants and hyperbolic cusp data
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the problem of connecting classical/gauge-theoretic knot invariants with hyperbolic geometry by defining the knot signature and the cusp longitude-meridian data of a hyperbolic knot complement.

proof_stages.label: Natural slope and theorem targets
proof_stages.summary: Machine-learning and plotting evidence isolate cusp skewness as relevant, leading to the natural slope and to two precise theorems estimating the signature by half the slope, first with an injectivity-radius error and then with a volume-linear error plus a short-geodesic correction.

proof_stages.label: Gordon-Litherland spanning-surface formula
proof_stages.summary: The proof switches from oriented Seifert surfaces to possibly nonorientable spanning surfaces, using the Gordon-Litherland form and the boundary framing term to express the knot signature in a way that can be compared to natural slope.

proof_stages.label: Bounded triangulations and normal boundary slope
proof_stages.summary: A Voronoi/Thurston triangulation is built with the number of tetrahedra controlled by hyperbolic geometry, and in the cusped case the boundary slope lambda minus n mu, with n the closest even integer to the natural slope, is arranged as a tightly controlled normal curve.

proof_stages.label: Mod-2 normal spanning surface construction
proof_stages.summary: Because lambda minus n mu is mod-2 null-homologous in the knot complement, it bounds a normal spanning surface meeting each tetrahedron in at most one triangle or square.

proof_stages.label: Coarse signature-slope estimate
proof_stages.summary: The normal surface has Euler characteristic, first homology rank, and hence Gordon-Litherland signature bounded by the tetrahedron count, while its framing is n, giving the bound between the knot signature and one half of the natural slope.

proof_stages.label: Volume-linear estimate with short-geodesic correction
proof_stages.summary: Using the Margulis thick-thin decomposition, the argument triangulates only the thick part with O(volume) tetrahedra and accounts separately for thin tubes around short geodesics of odd linking number, producing the correction term and the refined volume-linear theorem.

examples.name: Empirical hyperbolic-knot scatter plots
examples.note: Plots of many knots, including all hyperbolic knots up to 16 crossings and random knots, reveal the skewness/signature correlation and then the near-linear relation between natural slope and signature.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the scatter plots by reading axes, interpreting clustering/sign correlations, and using the slope-versus-signature plot to formulate and revise conjectures

passes.reason: The scatter plots aggregate typical hyperbolic-knot data as evidence for a pattern, not as an obstruction.
the scatter plots are empirical samples of hyperbolic knots used as evidence for a correlation rather than to refute a claim
The scatter plots are empirical datasets used to suggest a signature–slope correlation, with no intrinsically special knot object singled out.
the scatter plots are empirical evidence for a slope-signature relationship, not a failure case or structurally special object.
The scatter plots are representative empirical data used to suggest a pattern, not to refute one.

examples.name: Highly twisted knots as counterexamples to the first guess
examples.note: Highly twisted knots are cited as counterexamples to the hoped-for half-slope estimate with merely volume-linear error, forcing the more careful theorem with short-geodesic correction.
examples.engagement_reason: highly twisted knots are cited as counterexamples to the initial linear-error guess, without constructing them or deriving the failure in detail

passes.reason: Highly twisted knots form a large-twist limiting family used to disprove the first linear-in-volume guess.

examples.name: Bounded-volume knots with unbounded crossing number
examples.note: In response to a question, these knots illustrate that hyperbolic volume and diagrammatic crossing complexity correlate only statistically, warning that machine learning may find average rather than universal patterns.
examples.engagement_reason: bounded-volume knots with unbounded crossing number are invoked as known phenomena in response to a question, without working through a construction or estimate

passes.reason: Knots with bounded volume but unbounded crossing number are an extreme family refuting any reverse volume-crossing bound.
